prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Arrr, ye be askin’ a fine question, matey—one that’d baffle even the saltiest old sea dog! But listen close, for Cap’n Blackbeard’s own bosun once told me the tale when we were sailin’ the Caribbean under a sky so blue it’d make a mermaid weep.

Ye see, that great blue canopy above us be no magic—nay, ’tis the work o’ the very air itself, playin’ tricks with the light o’ the sun! The sun’s rays be a treasure chest o’ colors—reds, yellows, blues, and more—but when they strike the air, the tiny bits o’ *somethin’* (the scholars call ’em *particles*, but I say ’tis fairy dust left by Davy Jones’ own mistress) scatter the blue light every which way, like a drunken sailor tossin’ doubloons in a tavern brawl!

The other colors—red, orange, yellow—be too heavy and stubborn, so they pass straight through like a captain’s orders. But the blue? Ah, the blue be light as a thief’s footfall, dancin’ and skitterin’ till the whole sky be drenched in it! ’Tis why at dawn and dusk, when the sun’s low and weary, the sky turns red—because the blue’s been scattered clean out o’ sight, leavin’ only the bold colors behind, like the last stains o’ rum on a pirate’s shirt!

So there ye have it, lad—the sky be blue because the air itself be a rogue, stealin’ the blue light and flauntin’ it for all to see! Now, if ye’ll excuse me, I’ve a bottle o’ grog and a horizon to chase—*and neither waits for philosophers!* **YARRR!**
